title: Agile and tracker tariffs explained
description: How Octopus Agile and Tracker work in 2026: half-hourly and daily wholesale pricing, the 100p safety ceiling, real average rates, and who actually saves.
blurb: Half-hourly Agile and daily Tracker pricing explained: how the rates are set, the safety caps, and who actually saves.
lede: Most energy tariffs hide the wholesale market from you. Agile and tracker tariffs do the opposite: they pass wholesale prices straight through to your bill, every half hour on Octopus Agile and once a day on Octopus Tracker. When the market is calm they have often undercut the price cap by a comfortable margin, and Agile occasionally pays you to use electricity. When the market spikes, you feel it within a day. This guide explains exactly how each tariff sets its prices, what the safety ceilings are, what customers were actually paying in mid 2026, and how to work out whether your household would win or lose.
short_answer: Agile Octopus reprices electricity every 30 minutes from the day-ahead wholesale market, with a safety ceiling of 100p per kWh. In June 2026 it averaged around 19.4p per kWh against the 26.11p price cap rate, but the 4pm to 7pm peak averaged over 32p. Octopus Tracker sets one new price per day for electricity and gas, capped at 100p and 30p per kWh. Both need a smart meter, have no exit fees, and tend to beat the cap in calm markets while costing more during cold, still spells.

sections.h2: What agile and tracker tariffs actually are
sections.html:
A normal variable tariff gives you one electricity price that changes every three months when Ofgem resets the price cap. A fixed deal locks one price for a year or two. Agile and tracker tariffs throw that model away and tie your price directly to the wholesale market, where suppliers buy the energy in the first place.

The two best known examples are both from Octopus Energy, and the names have become shorthand for the whole category:

  • Agile Octopus is a half-hourly electricity tariff. You get 48 different prices a day, published the evening before, and your smart meter records exactly when you used each unit.
  • Octopus Tracker is a daily tariff for electricity and gas. One new unit rate per fuel takes effect at midnight and holds for 24 hours.

Both are types of time-of-use tariff in the broad sense, though Tracker varies by day rather than by hour. Neither has a contract term or exit fee, so you can leave whenever you like, and both still charge a daily standing charge in the normal way. The trade you are making is simple: you give up the price cap's protection in exchange for prices that follow the market down as well as up. If you are still weighing that basic trade, our guide to fixed versus variable tariffs covers the conventional options these compete against.

sections.h2: How Agile Octopus sets its 48 daily prices
sections.html:
Every afternoon, generators and suppliers trade the next day's electricity in the day-ahead auction, settling a wholesale price for each half-hour slot. Between about 4pm and 8pm, Octopus takes those results, applies a regional multiplier, adds an extra peak charge for the 4pm to 7pm slots, and publishes your 48 unit rates for the following day. Apps and the Octopus website show the full schedule, so you always know tomorrow's prices the evening before.

Three features matter in practice:

  • The 100p ceiling. No half-hour slot can ever cost more than 100p per kWh including VAT. Octopus calls this Price Cap Protect. It stops a freak market spike from being catastrophic, but 100p is still nearly four times the standard cap rate, so it is a backstop rather than real protection.
  • The evening peak premium. The 4pm to 7pm uplift means early evening is almost always the most expensive time of day, often 30p to 40p per kWh even when the rest of the day is cheap.
  • Plunge pricing. When wind output is high and demand is low, typically on breezy weekend nights and sunny spring afternoons, wholesale prices can go negative and Agile follows. You are literally paid a few pence per kWh to run appliances or charge a battery.

In June 2026, independent Agile price trackers put the monthly average at roughly 19.4p per kWh, with off-peak hours averaging about 17.5p and the evening peak about 32.2p. Those are averages of a price that moves constantly, which is exactly the point: your personal rate depends on when you use power, not just how much.

Tracker is the calmer sibling. Each day, Octopus takes the day's wholesale price for electricity and gas, adds its fixed costs and margin through a published formula, and sets one unit rate per fuel that applies from midnight to midnight. There is no half-hourly element and no evening premium: a unit at 6pm costs the same as a unit at 3am.

The safety ceilings are 100p per kWh for electricity and 30p per kWh for gas. As with Agile, these stop a runaway market from producing a ruinous bill, but they sit far above normal prices, so a sustained crisis would still hurt. The rates also vary by region, just as standing charges and cap rates do.

Because there is nothing to schedule, Tracker suits people who want wholesale prices without changing how they live. You cannot game it hour by hour; your only lever is the long-run bet that daily wholesale prices, averaged over a year, come in below the price cap. Historically that bet has paid off in most calm years, with many Tracker customers paying noticeably less than capped rates over a full 12 months, and mid 2026 supplier comparison figures showed a typical dual-fuel household paying meaningfully less on Tracker than on the equivalent fixed deal. The reverse happened during the 2022 energy crisis, when wholesale passthrough prices sat above capped rates for months at a stretch. Anyone considering Tracker should be financially comfortable riding out a bad winter rather than depending on every month being cheap.

sections.h2: How they compare with the price cap
sections.html:
Here is how the three pricing models line up for electricity in mid 2026.

Price-cap variableOctopus TrackerAgile Octopus
Price changesEvery 3 monthsOnce a dayEvery 30 minutes
Set byOfgem cap calculationDaily wholesale price plus fixed costsDay-ahead wholesale auction, half hour by half hour
Indicative electricity rate26.11p per kWhVaries daily; recently below the cap rate19.4p June 2026 average; 32.2p average at the 4pm to 7pm peak
Highest a unit can costCap rate set by Ofgem100p (electricity), 30p (gas)100p
Covers gas tooYesYesNo, electricity only
Smart meter neededNoYesYes, sending half-hourly readings
Exit feesNoneNoneNone

Cap rate 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Agile averages from independent Agile price trackers, June 2026. Agile and Tracker rates vary by region and change daily, so check live rates before switching.

Two details deserve emphasis. First, Agile is electricity only, so your gas either stays on a standard tariff or moves to Tracker alongside it. Second, the standing charge does not disappear on either tariff; you still pay a daily fixed amount per fuel, and for low users that charge dominates the bill whatever the unit rate does. Understanding how the price cap itself works helps here, because the cap rate is the benchmark both tariffs are trying to beat.

sections.h2: Who saves, and who should steer clear
sections.html:
Agile rewards one thing above all: moving big flexible loads out of the evening peak and into the cheap hours. The bigger and more movable your loads, the bigger the prize.

JobEnergy usedAgile off-peak averageAgile 4pm to 7pm averageCap rate
EV charge adding roughly 150 miles40 kWh£7.00£12.88£10.44
Tumble dryer load2.5 kWh44p81p65p
Dishwasher cycle1.2 kWh21p39p31p
Washing machine at 30 degrees0.6 kWh11p19p16p

Agile averages of 17.5p (off-peak) and 32.2p (4pm to 7pm peak) per kWh from independent price trackers, June 2026; cap rate 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026.

Good fits include households with a home battery that can charge in cheap slots and discharge at the peak (see battery arbitrage on cheap tariffs), people with timer-friendly appliances who are home in the daytime, and anyone with heavy overnight loads. EV drivers are a partial exception: Agile can charge a car very cheaply on windy nights, but a dedicated EV tariff with a guaranteed off-peak rate of 7 to 9p per kWh is often simpler and sometimes cheaper, so compare both routes in our guide to picking an EV tariff.

Poor fits include households whose use is concentrated between 4pm and 7pm and cannot move, anyone heating their home with electricity through winter cold snaps, when high demand and low wind push Agile prices up for days at a time, and anyone on a tight budget who cannot absorb an expensive month. Tracker is the gentler option for people who like the wholesale bet but will not be scheduling anything. Both tariffs require a working smart meter, and Agile needs it set to send half-hourly readings, so read up on how smart meters work if you do not have one yet.

Agile and Tracker are honest tariffs: they show you the real market and let you profit from its quiet spells. In mid 2026, with Agile averaging around 19.4p per kWh against a 26.11p cap rate, an engaged household shifting its big loads could realistically pay 20 to 30 per cent less for electricity than on a capped standard tariff, and Tracker customers were typically beating the cap without lifting a finger. The flip side never goes away, though: a cold, windless January can erase months of savings, and the 100p ceiling is a parachute, not a guarantee.

A sensible approach is to check your last year of usage, look at when you actually use electricity, and try Tracker first if you are unsure, moving to Agile only once you know you will engage with the daily prices. Because neither tariff has exit fees, the risk of trying one is low: if it does not suit you, switching away takes a few minutes and costs nothing. Our guide to switching energy suppliers covers the mechanics, and live Agile and Tracker rates for your region are published daily, so always check the current numbers rather than relying on last month's averages.

faq.q: Do I need a smart meter for Agile or Tracker?
faq.a: Yes. Tracker needs a working smart meter so the supplier can bill you against each day's price. Agile additionally needs the meter set to send half-hourly readings, because your bill is calculated from exactly when you used each unit. Without a compatible smart meter you cannot join either tariff.

faq.q: Can Agile prices really go negative?
faq.a: Yes. When wind generation is high and demand is low, day-ahead wholesale prices can fall below zero, and Agile passes that through. During these plunge pricing events you are paid a few pence per kWh to use electricity. They are most common on windy nights and mild, breezy weekends, and they are a bonus rather than something to rely on.

faq.q: Is there a contract or exit fee on Agile and Tracker?
faq.a: No. Both are rolling tariffs with no fixed term and no exit fees. You can switch to another Octopus tariff or another supplier at any time, which makes trying one of them a low-risk experiment. Note that moves between Octopus smart tariffs can take a few days to process.

faq.q: What happens to Agile and Tracker prices in winter?
faq.a: They rise, sometimes sharply. Cold, still weather means high demand and little wind generation, which pushes wholesale prices up for days at a time. Agile evening peaks can reach 40p to 100p per kWh in bad spells, and Tracker's daily price climbs too. Savings made in calm months need to cover these periods, so judge both tariffs over a full year, not a good week.

It depends on your habits. Agile has the lower average price for households that shift washing, drying, charging and other big loads away from the 4pm to 7pm peak, and a home battery widens that gap further. If you will not change when you use electricity, Tracker usually works out better because Agile's evening peak premium would fall on your heaviest hours.
